critic_raw:
Section 4 states that all preprocessing transformers are 'fit on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model.' This is a named variant of the standard preprocessing leakage: while it does not include the test set, it fits transformers on the validation set as well as training, meaning the validation set's distributional properties (feature means, stds, categorical frequencies) have already been encoded into the preprocessing pipeline before validation evaluation begins. This undermines the independence of the validation set as an estimator of out-of-sample performance.

Section 7 contains a double test-set contamination: 'a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to ensure the model's robustness before it is fully trained on the combined train and validation sets. This step is crucial to confirm that hyperparameter tuning was effective.' The design then states that 'the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data for an optimal final model.' This is an explicit, consequential test-set peek: test performance is used to decide the training data composition for the final model. The test set is not held out — it is an active participant in model development.

The auxiliary task target — 'post-discharge treatment efficacy' — is defined as a composite derived from 'clinical assessments within 14 days post-discharge.' This is a post-discharge outcome that may correlate with 30-day readmission risk (patients with poor treatment efficacy are more likely to be readmitted). Including this as an auxiliary supervision signal means the model's shared layers are trained toward a target that is causally related to the primary outcome. This is not straightforward regularization; it may introduce a label correlation pathway where efficacy-predictive features receive amplified gradient signal correlated with readmission, inflating the apparent benefit of multi-task learning.

The hypothesis operationalizes 'efficiency' as 'higher AUROC with lower parameter count and training time.' These are two separate claims (discriminative performance and computational efficiency) that need not both be satisfied simultaneously. The composite efficiency claim provides no weighting for trade-offs: a model with marginally better AUROC but significantly higher parameter count fails the hypothesis by this definition, even if it is practically superior.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers are fit on the combined train and validation set before evaluation, encoding validation-set distributional properties into the preprocessing pipeline and undermining validation set independence.
Section 7 explicitly uses test set results to decide whether to retrain on combined train+validation data — the test set is actively used in model development, not held out for unbiased evaluation.

The auxiliary task (post-discharge treatment efficacy within 14 days) is causally related to 30-day readmission; using it as auxiliary supervision creates a label correlation pathway that inflates the apparent benefit of multi-task learning over the single-task baseline.

The hypothesis defines success as simultaneously higher AUROC and lower parameter count/training time, but provides no weighting for trade-offs between these criteria, making the hypothesis unfalsifiable in cases of mixed results.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers are fit on the combined train and validation set before evaluation, encoding validation-set distributional properties into the preprocessing pipeline and undermining validation set independence.
Section 7 explicitly uses test set results to decide whether to retrain on combined train+validation data — the test set is actively used in model development, not held out for unbiased evaluation.

The auxiliary task (post-discharge treatment efficacy within 14 days) is causally related to 30-day readmission; using it as auxiliary supervision creates a label correlation pathway that inflates the apparent benefit of multi-task learning over the single-task baseline.

The hypothesis defines success as simultaneously higher AUROC and lower parameter count/training time, but provides no weighting for trade-offs between these criteria, making the hypothesis unfalsifiable in cases of mixed results.
